Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'm 29, working part-time, and juggling a lot, so I couldn't really stick to a schedule. I'd grab time whenever I could—sometimes late at night, sometimes on my lunch break. I learn best by doing things with my hands, so I'd often print materials or write things out physically rather than just staring at screens. The 27 hours I put in each week were scattered all over the place.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then I'd have these intense cramming sessions right before deadlines. My attendance was really poor because I just couldn't make it to everything with my other responsibilities. I tried to set regular times but life kept getting in the way, and honestly, the anxiety made it hard to stick to plans.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being honest. I'd log in when I suddenly remembered something was due or when I got an email notification that scared me. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pull me in because they felt less overwhelming than regular assignments. The fear of falling behind was usually what got me to open that platform, not genuine curiosity or routine.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my day—it always felt like this looming thing I had to deal with. The gamified elements helped a bit because they made logging in feel less threatening, but even then, it never became automatic. I'd go days without logging in and then feel this wave of dread when I finally did.
Q5: The video lectures were actually really helpful because I could pause, rewind, and watch them while doing other things or taking physical notes. I watched a lot of video content—over two hours worth that I really engaged with. The gamified tasks also helped because they broke things into smaller pieces that felt manageable when my anxiety was high. Anything I could interact with physically or practically stuck better than just reading.
Q6: The discussion forums—I barely touched them. I only posted once the entire course. I felt too anxious to put my thoughts out there for everyone to see and judge. Reading through other people's discussions felt like a waste of time when I was already stretched so thin. I also ignored a lot of the supplementary readings because I just didn't have the bandwidth.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate, practical results or if it directly connected to the assignments. The gamified tasks worked because I could see progress and earn something tangible. When something felt abstract or disconnected from what I needed to pass, I couldn't justify spending my limited energy on it. I needed to feel like I was actually accomplishing something concrete.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With my anxiety levels and limited resources, I had to triage everything. Required activities got done because they had to—even if I was terrified the whole time. Optional stuff just fell off my radar completely because the fear of failing the required work consumed all my mental energy.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so I'd download everything I could and print it out. I'd work through problems by hand, make physical flashcards, and practice things away from the computer. A lot of my actual learning happened with printed notes spread across my kitchen table, not sitting in front of the LMS. That's how I managed to score well on assignments despite my low engagement online.
Q10: Most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download a lecture, watch it once on the platform, then review my handwritten notes for hours afterward. I'd practice problems on paper, talk through concepts out loud to myself while doing chores. None of that showed up anywhere. The LMS only saw me log in and download things—it didn't see the hours I spent actually wrestling with the material.
Q11: All the time. I'd access something once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never go back to the LMS version. My internet isn't always reliable with my limited resources, so working offline was more practical anyway. I'd have PDFs and notes saved on my phone that I'd review on the bus or during breaks at work. One view on the platform might represent days of offline study.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods. My anxiety would spike and I'd just freeze—I couldn't bring myself to log in because I was scared of what I'd find. Sometimes work would get intense and I'd have to choose between paying rent and doing coursework. The fear would build up the longer I stayed away, making it even harder to come back. It was this awful cycle.
Q13: Constantly. The stress and anxiety affected everything. Some days I was so mentally exhausted that I'd stare at the screen without absorbing anything. When work got demanding or money got tight, the LMS was the first thing to suffer. I'd log in intending to study but end up just clicking around because my brain was too fried to actually engage. The fatigue was real.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the videos playing but my mind would be somewhere else—worrying about bills, about failing, about everything. I'd scroll through materials without really reading them. Sometimes I logged in just to feel like I was doing something, even though I wasn't actually learning. The anxiety made it hard to be present.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even when I knew it would make things worse. The anxiety created this paralysis where the more important something was, the scarier it felt to start. I'd avoid assignments until the last possible moment, then somehow pull it together under pressure. It's exhausting and I know it's not healthy, but the fear was just overwhelming sometimes.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without them, I'd drift. I'd check the LMS obsessively as deadlines approached, terrified of missing something. My whole engagement pattern was driven by due dates—huge spikes of activity right before things were due, then nothing for days. It wasn't sustainable but it's how I survived.
Q17: Feedback was complicated. When I got good feedback, like on my assignments where I scored really well, it gave me a temporary boost. But I was also scared to check feedback because I dreaded criticism. Sometimes I'd delay looking at comments for days because I couldn't handle the potential disappointment. The fear of negative feedback sometimes kept me from engaging at all.
Q18: The structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having clear deadlines gave me something to work toward, but having 14 courses meant everything blurred together. The gamified elements helped break things down into less scary chunks. But overall, the sheer volume made it hard to engage deeply with anything—I was always just trying to keep my head above water.
Q19: The gamified tasks actually made things easier—they felt less intimidating than traditional assignments. But the discussion requirements were hard for me because of my anxiety around public participation. The course assuming constant internet access was also challenging given my situation. More flexibility and less emphasis on visible participation would have helped someone like me.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my anxious days—logging in repeatedly, clicking around, trying to engage but not being able to actually produce anything. Fear can make you circle around something without ever landing. Or maybe they're trying to understand the material but struggling to translate that into submitted work. High activity doesn't mean high productivity when you're scared.
Q21: That's basically my story. My LMS activity was low, but I scored 98 on assignments and got a Merit overall. I did most of my real learning offline, with printed materials and handwritten notes. The platform only captured a fraction of my actual engagement with the content. Someone can be deeply engaged with learning without being visibly engaged with the LMS.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone logs in and what they click on, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss all the offline work, the mental processing, the anxiety that keeps someone from engaging visibly. They can't capture someone printing materials and studying for hours, or the fear that makes logging in feel impossible. They show behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually wrestling with the material, trying to understand it, caring about learning something real. It's not about how many times you log in or how many posts you make. For me, engagement happened mostly in my head and on paper, not on the platform. Real engagement is when the content matters to you, even when you're scared.
Q24: The LMS can't show my anxiety, my fear, or how hard I fought to keep going despite feeling terrified. It can't show the hours I spent with printed notes, the concepts I talked through while washing dishes, or the relief when something finally clicked. It can't show that I'm resilient—that despite everything working against me, I still earned a Merit. That's the real story it misses.
